skill.name: face-swap
skill.description: Swap a face or character into video or images on RunComfy via the runcomfy CLI. Routes across multiple AI models (Wan 2-2 Animate, GPT Image 2 Edit, Nano Banana Edit, Flux Kontext, Kling Motion Control) based on user intent.
skill.summary: Swap faces or characters into video or images using RunComfy's AI model routing.
skill.author: doany-ai

security_audit.summary: Static analysis detected 129 potential issues, but evaluation reveals all are false positives. The skill is a legitimate RunComfy CLI wrapper for AI face-swapping models with documented security controls including scope-limited Bash access, JSON-based input handling to prevent shell injection, explicit consent/refusal guidance for harmful requests, and an outbound endpoint allowlist.

medium_findings.title: Extensive Shell Command Invocation
medium_findings.description: The skill invokes the runcomfy CLI with various model endpoints. While scope-limited to Bash(runcomfy *), the volume of command examples could present risk if input validation fails.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: All 76 'external_commands' detections are markdown code blocks documenting CLI usage, not actual code execution. The skill explicitly declares allowed-tools: Bash(runcomfy *) limiting scope.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded External URLs
low_findings.description: Documentation contains hardcoded URLs to runcomfy.com services.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All 39 'Hardcoded URL' detections are legitimate RunComfy service URLs in documentation context. No credential exfiltration risk identified.

low_findings.title: Token Storage in Home Directory
low_findings.description: The skill documents storing API tokens at ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Intentional configuration documentation with explicit mode 0600 permission requirement. CI alternative via RUNCOMFY_TOKEN env var is documented.

content.user_title: Swap faces in images and videos with AI routing
content.value_statement: Creating face-swapped content requires choosing the right AI model for your specific use case. This skill routes across RunComfy's face-swap models to pick the optimal approach based on whether you need video or stills, motion preservation, or batch processing.
actual_capabilities: Swap faces in still images using GPT Image 2 Edit with up to 10 reference images
Generate video with face swap using Wan 2-2 Animate with audio-driven animation
Transfer motion from one video performance to a target character using Kling Motion Control
Apply batch identity-preserving face swaps across multiple images using Nano Banana Edit
Edit a single image face precisely using Flux Kontext Pro with text descriptions
limitations: Cannot perform face swaps without rights to both the target face and source content
Single image edits per call for Flux Kontext; batch requires multiple passes
Audio-driven animation requires a separate audio track input

use_cases.title: Create consistent brand ambassador content across product shots
use_cases.description: Marketing teams can use Nano Banana Edit to swap the same spokesperson's face into multiple product images while preserving consistency.

use_cases.title: Animate a character with a target identity and voice
use_cases.description: Video producers can use Wan 2-2 Animate to create dubbed content where a character's face is swapped and synced to a new audio track.

use_cases.title: Replace an actor in an existing video while preserving motion
use_cases.description: Filmmakers can use Kling Motion Control to transfer the exact motion and blocking from one performance onto a different character.

prompt_templates.title: Basic face swap in a still image
prompt_templates.prompt: Swap the face in [describe image] with the face from [describe reference]. Preserve pose, lighting, and background.
prompt_templates.scenario: When user wants a single face swap in a photograph or illustration

prompt_templates.title: Video character swap with audio
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a video using [reference image] as the character and [audio file] as the voice track. The character should speak the audio naturally.
prompt_templates.scenario: When user provides a portrait and audio to create an animated character

prompt_templates.title: Motion transfer to new character
prompt_templates.prompt: Take the motion from [source video] and apply it to [character image]. Preserve the performance exactly while changing the character.
prompt_templates.scenario: When user wants to transfer specific movements to a different identity

prompt_templates.title: Batch face swap across multiple scenes
prompt_templates.prompt: Replace the face in each of these images with [identity description]. Keep pose, clothing, and lighting consistent. Use the first image as the identity reference.
prompt_templates.scenario: When user needs the same face across multiple images or frames

output_examples.input: Swap the face from the portrait into the group photo, preserving the background and other people's faces
output_examples.output: Successfully processed. The portrait subject's face has been composited into the group photo. The background and other faces remain unchanged. Output saved to ./out/result.jpg

output_examples.input: Create a video of this cartoon character speaking with the audio track
output_examples.output: Processing audio-driven animation. The character will drive based on the audio rhythm and content. Estimated generation time: 2-5 minutes. Check ./out/ for the completed video.

output_examples.input: Transfer the dance moves from this video onto the mascot character
output_examples.output: Motion captured from source performance and applied to mascot character. The exact blocking and timing is preserved. Output saved to ./out/result.mp4

best_practices: Use high-quality, well-lit reference images with clear frontal faces for best swap results
Match lighting conditions between source and target images to avoid the 'floating face' effect
Always verify you have rights to use both the target face and the source content before swapping
anti_patterns: Do not attempt to swap faces of real individuals without their explicit consent
Avoid using low-resolution or blurry reference images which degrade output quality
Do not ignore the skill's consent warnings for defamatory, explicit, or harmful synthetic media

faq.question: What is face swapping?
faq.answer: Face swapping replaces one person's face with another in images or videos using AI models. This skill routes across multiple RunComfy models to pick the best approach for your specific use case.

faq.question: What models does this skill use?
faq.answer: The skill routes between Wan 2-2 Animate, GPT Image 2 Edit, Nano Banana Edit, Flux Kontext Pro, and Kling Motion Control Pro based on whether you need video or stills, motion preservation, or batch processing.

faq.question: Can I use this to swap real people's faces?
faq.answer: You must have rights to both the target face and source content. The skill includes consent warnings and instructs refusal of harmful requests involving real people without consent.

faq.question: What is the difference between the video models?
faq.answer: Wan 2-2 Animate uses audio to drive character animation. Kling Motion Control preserves exact motion from a source video. Choose based on whether you need audio-sync or motion preservation.

faq.question: How do I get the best results?
faq.answer: Use high-quality, well-lit reference images with clear frontal faces. Match lighting conditions between source and target. For batch work, lock aspect ratio and resolution settings.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to use?
faq.answer: The skill has documented security controls including scope-limited CLI access, JSON-based input handling to prevent injection, and explicit consent requirements. Review the Consent section before use.
